stimuli.caption: The chart, which is a combination of a bar chart and a line chart, includes legends identifying "Accurate" in pink and "Inaccurate" in cyan. Although the labels are not specified, the data comparison illustrates that the "Inaccurate" bar values are higher than the "Accurate" bar values in all categories except for the last category (1.0), where the "Accurate" bar slightly exceeds the "Inaccurate" bar. The blue line, likely representing "Inaccurate," decreases gradually across the categories, whereas the red line, likely representing "Accurate," shows a progressive increase, with both lines converging at the last category (1.0). The categories range from 0.3 to 1.0, with corresponding values for the "Accurate" series of approximately 16% at 0.3, 15% at 0.4, 18% at 0.5, 22% at 0.6, 25% at 0.7, 28% at 0.8, 35% at 0.9, and 60% at 1.0, while the "Inaccurate" series has values of approximately 84% at 0.3, 85% at 0.4, 82% at 0.5, 78% at 0.6, 75% at 0.7, 72% at 0.8, 65% at 0.9, and 40% at 1.0. The minimum value for the "Accurate" series occurs at approximately 16% at 0.3, while the maximum is around 60% at 1.0. In contrast, the "Inaccurate" series has a minimum of approximately 40% at 1.0 and a maximum of around 84% at 0.3. Both series exhibit a range of about 44 percentage points, with the "Accurate" series ranging from approximately 16% to 60% and the "Inaccurate" series ranging from approximately 40% to 84%. The X-axis is labeled as "Consistency Quantification," ranging from 0.3 to 1.0 in 0.1 intervals, and the Y-axis represents percentage, ranging from 0% to 80% in 20% intervals.

The scatter plot features blue circles labeled "TZ from u to ỹ" and red squares labeled "TZ from u to ẑ." Both the blue circle and red square coincide at the point (1, 0), which also represents the only data point for the blue circles. The red squares have additional points at (0, 0) and (3, 0), with all points being discrete and primarily aligned along the real-axis (Re(s)), showing sparse or no noticeable spread along the imaginary-axis (Im(s)). The x-axis ranges approximately from -1 to 3.5, while the y-axis ranges approximately from -0.5 to 0.5. The leftmost point for the red squares is at (0, 0), and the rightmost point is at (3, 0). There is one cluster at (1, 0) where both series overlap, and no anomalies were detected as all points follow a regular pattern along the x-axis at y=0. The data range for "TZ from u to ỹ" is a single point at 0, while for "TZ from u to ẑ," it is also at 0, with all points lying on the x-axis. No trends or correlations are specified in the chart information.

The line chart, whose title is not specified, presents data series identified by legends: "DCT" (blue), "Random" (orange), "OptSingle" (red), and "Coloring" (green). The average computation time for "OptSingle" (red) is the highest across all node numbers, while "Random" (orange) consistently exhibits the lowest average computation time. The average computation times for "DCT" (blue) and "Coloring" (green) fall between those of "Random" and "OptSingle." As the number of nodes increases from 10 to 25, the gap between "OptSingle" and the other series progressively widens. There is a noticeable increase in average computation time for all data series with rising node numbers, with the increase being more pronounced for "OptSingle" compared to the other three categories. The X-axis represents the "Number of Nodes," ranging from 10 to 25, while the Y-axis denotes "Average Computation Time" on a log scale, ranging approximately from 10^-3 to 10^1. Specific values for the DCT series show an initial computation time of approximately 0.005 at 10 nodes, a middle value of approximately 0.012 at 17.5 nodes, and an end value of approximately 0.025 at 25 nodes. The Random series starts with approximately 0.0005 at 10 nodes, reaches approximately 0.002 at 17.5 nodes, and ends at approximately 0.01 at 25 nodes. For the OptSingle series, the computation time starts at approximately 0.007 at 10 nodes, rises to approximately 0.1 at 17.5 nodes, and peaks at approximately 15.0 at 25 nodes. The Coloring series begins at approximately 0.006 at 10 nodes, increases to approximately 0.02 at 17.5 nodes, and culminates at approximately 0.05 at 25 nodes. The minimum values for DCT, Random, OptSingle, and Coloring occur at (10, ~0.005), (10, ~0.0005), (10, ~0.007), and (10, ~0.006), respectively, while their maximum values are (25, ~0.025), (25, ~0.01), (25, ~15.0), and (25, ~0.05). The ranges of average computation times are approximately ~0.005 to ~0.025 for DCT, ~0.0005 to ~0.01 for Random, ~0.007 to ~15.0 for OptSingle, and ~0.006 to ~0.05 for Coloring. The chart does not specify any labels.

The bar chart compares the performance of three methods: LS (blue), TLS (orange), and EGLE (green) under two categories: Under Gaussian noise and Under GMM noise. In the "Under Gaussian noise" category, the values for LS are approximately 0.17, TLS are approximately 0.15, and EGLE are approximately 0.05, with the bars for LS, TLS, and EGLE showing relatively close values and minimal differences. In contrast, in the "Under GMM noise" category, the values for LS are approximately 0.75, TLS are approximately 0.68, and EGLE are approximately 0.10, where LS and TLS bars have significantly higher values compared to EGLE, with LS being slightly higher than TLS. The Y-axis represents the r - MARE (%) ranging from 0 to 0.8 (or 0% to 80%). The minimum and maximum values for each method are: LS has a minimum of 0.17 (Gaussian) and a maximum of 0.75 (GMM), TLS has a minimum of 0.15 (Gaussian) and a maximum of 0.68 (GMM), while EGLE has a minimum of 0.05 (Gaussian) and a maximum of 0.10 (GMM). The ranges calculated are: LS with a range of 0.58 (0.75 - 0.17), TLS with a range of 0.53 (0.68 - 0.15), and EGLE with a range of 0.05 (0.10 - 0.05).

The chart, which is a line chart, includes a legend differentiating six data series labeled as Maxmin (ε = 0), Maxmin (ε = 0.2), Maxmin (ε = 0.4), Maxexp (ε = 0), Maxexp (ε = 0.2), and Maxexp (ε = 0.4). While the labels are not specified, the data comparison indicates that among the six data series, there are noticeable similarities across many points, particularly beyond approximately 5000 queries, where all curves converge close to an AMI score of 1.0; differences are more pronounced in the earlier sections, up to about 2500 queries. The data correlations or trends show that all series demonstrate a positive correlation between the number of queries and the AMI score, following a non-linear upward curve that levels off near the maximum AMI score of 1. The X-axis represents the "Number of queries," ranging from 0 to 12500, while the Y-axis represents the "AMI Score," ranging from 0.0 to 1.0. The initial points for all series start around (0, 0.35), the middle points are around (5000, 0.85), and the end points converge to (12500, 1.0). The minimum value for all series is approximately (0, 0.35), whereas the maximum is approximately (12500, 1.0). The range of the Y-axis data points spans from 0.35 to 1.0, with an approximate span of 0.65.

The bar chart titled "How many items per cluster" displays various topics and motivations associated with the dark web, labeled on the y-axis, while the x-axis represents the number of items, ranging from 0 to 200. The category "dark-web:motivation='marketplace-for-sale'" has the largest value at approximately 190 items, significantly higher than other categories such as "dark-web:motivation='forum'" with around 125 items, "dark-web:topic='drugs-narcotics'" with roughly 115 items, and "dark-web:topic='unclear'" with about 105 items. Other categories show decreasing values with a notable drop compared to the highest category, including "dark-web:topic='finance'" at around 95 items, "dark-web:topic='search-engine-index'" at approximately 90 items, "dark-web:motivation='education-training'" at about 85 items, "dark-web:topic='legitimate'" with roughly 80 items, "dark-web:topic='information-sharing-reportage'" at around 75 items, and "dark-web:topic='hacking'" with about 65 items. The minimum value is around 5 items for multiple categories, including "dark-web:topic='cash-in'", "gambling", and "recruitment-advocacy", contributing to a total range of approximately 185 items across 38 distinct categories. Information regarding specific categories and legends is not provided.

The chart titled "Protein Structure" is a line chart that includes legends explaining the symbols and colors corresponding to each data series: HB, RS, BOHB, TPE, SMAC, RE, RL, and Bohamiann. There are no specific labels annotating individual elements. The chart represents each line as a unique category's cumulative density function (CDF) as a function of final test regret, where variations between series can be observed. Bohamiann reaches the highest CDF values the fastest, while HB shows significantly lower CDF compared to others up to a similar value of final test regret. Bohamiann demonstrates rapid growth in CDF at low levels of final test regret, while the other methods (HB, SMAC, RL) exhibit slower improvements, with RS and BOHB showing intermediate performance. The x-axis represents "final test regret" on a logarithmic scale with a range of approximately 5x10^-4 to 3x10^-2, and the y-axis represents "CDF" on a linear scale ranging from 0 to 1.0. The retrieved values for HB are (5x10^-4, 0.05), (8x10^-3, 0.5), (2x10^-2, 1.0); for RS are (5x10^-4, 0.01), (8x10^-3, 0.2), (2x10^-2, 1.0); for BOHB are (5x10^-4, 0.1), (3x10^-3, 0.35), (1.5x10^-2, 1.0); for TPE are (5x10^-4, 0.37), (3x10^-3, 0.68), (1x10^-2, 0.95); for SMAC are (5x10^-4, 0.2), (3x10^-3, 0.4), (1.5x10^-2, 0.9); for RE are (5x10^-4, 0.2), (3x10^-3, 0.6), (1x10^-2, 0.95); for RL are (5x10^-4, 0.01), (8x10^-3, 0.2), (2x10^-2, 1.0); and for Bohamiann are (5x10^-4, 0.75), (3x10^-3, 0.8), (1x10^-2, 0.95). The extremum values identified are Min(5x10^-4, 0.05) and Max(2x10^-2, 1.0) for HB; Min(5x10^-4, 0.01) and Max(2x10^-2, 1.0) for RS; Min(5x10^-4, 0.1) and Max(1.5x10^-2, 1.0) for BOHB; Min(5x10^-4, 0.37) and Max(1x10^-2, 0.95) for TPE; Min(5x10^-4, 0.2) and Max(1.5x10^-2, 0.9) for SMAC; Min(5x10^-4, 0.2) and Max(1x10^-2, 0.95) for RE; Min(5x10^-4, 0.01) and Max(2x10^-2, 1.0) for RL; and Min(5x10^-4, 0.75) and Max(1x10^-2, 0.95) for Bohamiann. The ranges are 0.05 to 1.0 (range: 0.95) for HB; 0.01 to 1.0 (range: 0.99) for RS; 0.1 to 1.0 (range: 0.9) for BOHB; 0.37 to 0.95 (range: 0.58) for TPE; 0.2 to 0.9 (range: 0.7) for SMAC; 0.2 to 0.95 (range: 0.75) for RE; 0.01 to 1.0 (range: 0.99) for RL; and 0.75 to 0.95 (range: 0.2) for Bohamiann.

The bar chart illustrates five categories: Squared, Tanh, Tuner, Time25, and Time50, with numerical labels above the bars. The X-axis presents five groups categorized as Square, 1-rdg, HMtrx, ttm50, and ttm60, while the Y-axis utilizes a logarithmic scale ranging from approximately 1 to 2000. The data comparison highlights evident differences between categories, showcasing visible variations in bar heights, particularly noting that the "Tanh" bar consistently appears taller compared to others in paired groupings. The values for each group are as follows: Group 1 features Square=85, 1-rdg=1700, HMtrx=0, ttm50=0, ttm60=0; Group 2 shows Square=100, 1-rdg=160, HMtrx=45, ttm50=0, ttm60=0; Group 3 includes Square=100, 1-rdg=23, HMtrx=1200, ttm50=0, ttm60=0; Group 4 lists Square=90, 1-rdg=23, HMtrx=800, ttm50=0, ttm60=0; and Group 5 specifies Square=91, 1-rdg=41, HMtrx=0, ttm50=0, ttm60=0. The minimum value observed is 23, occurring in the 1-rdg of Groups 3 and 4, while the maximum value is 1700, found in the 1-rdg of Group 1, resulting in a range of 1677 from the minimum to the maximum. Additionally, the chart does not specify a title or legends.

The line chart presents a comparison between two categories of Algorithm 1: "Algorithm 1 with power saturation constraint," represented by the blue line, and "Algorithm 1 without power saturation constraint," represented by the red line. For the same ET transmit power, the series for "Algorithm 1 with power saturation constraint" consistently shows higher values for the sum of the output power compared to the series for "Algorithm 1 without power saturation constraint." Both lines demonstrate an increasing trend, suggesting a positive correlation between ET transmit power and the sum of the output power for both algorithms. The X-axis represents ET transmit power (W) within a range of 2 to 12 W, while the Y-axis indicates the sum of the output power (mW) ranging from 0.44 to 0.58 mW. The data points for "Algorithm 1 with power saturation constraint" are (2, 0.455), (7, 0.53), and (12, 0.57), with a minimum of (2, 0.455) and a maximum of (12, 0.57), resulting in a range of 0.115 mW. In contrast, the data points for "Algorithm 1 without power saturation constraint" are (2, 0.445), (7, 0.52), and (12, 0.555), with a minimum of (2, 0.445) and a maximum of (12, 0.555), resulting in a range of 0.11 mW. Notably, information regarding the chart title and labels is not provided.

The scatter plot, which does not have a specified title or legends, features labeled points A, B, C, D, and E positioned around specific clusters or points. The data points are represented by both filled and open markers, indicating a categorical or differentiating variable, with some clusters, such as the one around point A, being denser, while point E appears more isolated. The distribution of the data is irregular, showing clustering of points but lacking a clear overall pattern. The X-axis represents relative galactic longitude in degrees, ranging approximately from -1 to 1, while the Y-axis represents relative galactic latitude in degrees, ranging approximately from -0.4 to 0.4. Points B, D, and E are labeled on the axes, with point E located at approximately (0.0, 0.3), B at approximately (0.0, 0.15), and D at approximately (0.0, -0.3). Initial points are plotted at (0.8, 0.0) and (-0.9, 0.2), the middle region point is (0.0, 0.0), and end points include (-0.8, 0.1) and (0.9, -0.1). The maximum latitude is approximately 0.35 degrees, while the minimum latitude is approximately -0.35 degrees, indicating a latitude range of approximately 0.7 degrees. The maximum longitude is approximately 0.9 degrees, and the minimum longitude is approximately -0.9 degrees, resulting in a longitude range of approximately 1.8 degrees. There is a dense cluster near (0,0), a small cluster near E at (0.0, 0.3), and another near D at (0.0, -0.3). Notably, points near E appear slightly isolated from the main distribution, and there are a few scattered points at the extremes of longitude values. Data correlations and trends are not specified.

The bar chart titled "Top3_ACC on MOOC" displays the ACC_3 values across three size categories: 8, 16, and 32, with the x-axis labeled as "Size Number" and the y-axis showing ACC_3 values ranging approximately from 0.70 to 0.76. The data reveals that the bar for category 16 is higher than both category 8 and category 32, while the bar for category 8 is the lowest at 0.715. Both category 16 and category 32 share the maximum ACC_3 value of 0.741, resulting in a minimum ACC_3 of 0.715 for category 8 and a calculated range of 0.026 from 0.715 to 0.741. No additional labels or legends are provided in the chart.

The chart titled "pGMM Kernel Regression" is a line chart that illustrates the MSE (Mean Squared Error) values for different values of \(p\) (specifically, \(p = 80\), \(p = 60\), \(p = 40\), and \(p = 20\)), where the X-axis represents \(\lambda\) on a logarithmic scale from \(10^{-5}\) to \(10^{0}\) and the Y-axis depicts MSE on a linear scale from 0.011 to 0.016. The data series exhibit different MSE values, noting that higher values of \(p\) result in larger MSE deviations at higher \(\lambda\) values (e.g., near \(10^{0}\)), while their values are nearly parallel in the lower \(\lambda\) range. Each data series demonstrates a U-shaped pattern, indicating that MSE decreases initially as \(\lambda\) increases, reaches a minimum, and then rises again, consistent across all values of \(p\) with varying magnitudes of deviation. At initial points where \(\lambda = 10^{-5}\), the MSE values are approximately 0.0136 for \(p=80\), 0.0129 for \(p=60\), 0.0125 for \(p=40\), and 0.0131 for \(p=20\). At middle points where \(\lambda = 10^{-2}\), the values are approximately 0.0137 for \(p=80\), 0.0130 for \(p=60\), 0.0126 for \(p=40\), and 0.0132 for \(p=20\). At the end points where \(\lambda = 10^{0}\), all series converge to approximately 0.016. The minimum MSE values occur at \(\lambda = 10^{-5}\) with the corresponding values for \(p=80\) at 0.0136, \(p=20\) at 0.0131, \(p=60\) at 0.0129, and \(p=40\) at 0.0125, while the maximum MSE for all series is approximately 0.016 at \(\lambda = 10^{0}\). The ranges of MSE values for each \(p\) are as follows: for \(p=80\), it ranges from 0.0136 to 0.016 (range: 0.0024), for \(p=20\), it ranges from 0.0131 to 0.016 (range: 0.0029), for \(p=60\), it ranges from 0.0129 to 0.016 (range: 0.0031), and for \(p=40\), it ranges from 0.0125 to 0.016 (range: 0.0035). Legends are not specified in the chart.

The scatter plot demonstrates a negative correlation between the x-axis variable \( T \) (ranging from 0 to 4 K) and the y-axis variable \( g^* \) (ranging from 2.5 to 4.5), showing that as \( T \) increases, \( g^* \) decreases. The data points begin at an initial value of (0.5, 4.0), decrease through a middle point of (2.0, 3.2), and end at (4.0, 2.9). The maximum value is identified at point (0.5, 4.0) and the minimum at (4.0, 2.9). The y-axis data range is 1.1, calculated from the values 2.9 to 4.0. Throughout the chart, the data points exhibit a clustered arrangement in a decreasing trend with no apparent outliers or distinct clusters, and no significant anomalies are present, as the points consistently adhere to the downward trend.

The scatter plot presents four categories of data identified by the legend: black squares represent PRIMUS quiescent galaxies, magenta stars denote PRIMUS SF galaxies, orange stars signify PRIMUS SF (with M* selection), and cyan circles indicate QSO–galaxy (with M* selection). The orange stars, magenta stars, and cyan circles tend to overlap or cluster closely around similar \(R_\perp\) and median \(W_{2796}\) values, while the black squares are separated and positioned lower on the \(W_{2796}\) scale. Notably, the cyan circles display the largest horizontal and vertical error bars compared to other categories. The data points do not form a clear overall shape but indicate clustering around higher values of \(R_\perp\) for most categories, with the black squares occupying lower \(W_{2796}\) values. There is no apparent strong correlation between \(R_\perp\) (x-axis) and median \(W_{2796}\) (y-axis) across all categories, although the overlap among some datasets suggests potential similarities in trends for certain categories, while the black squares diverge. The x-axis, representing \(R_\perp\), is on a logarithmic scale from approximately 10 to 100 kpc, and the y-axis, representing median \(W_{2796}\), is on a linear scale from 0 to 2.0 Å. The data series includes PRIMUS quiescent galaxies (black squares), PRIMUS SF galaxies (magenta stars), PRIMUS SF (w/M* selection) (orange stars), and QSO-galaxy (w/M* selection) (cyan circles). The values for PRIMUS quiescent galaxies are (40, 0.15); for PRIMUS SF galaxies: (15, 0.9), (40, 0.2), (100, 0.2); for PRIMUS SF (w/M* selection): (15, 0.95), (40, 0.2), (100, 0.2); and for QSO-galaxy (w/M* selection): (15, 0.8), (40, 0.4), (100, 0.2). The minimum observed value is (40, 0.15) from PRIMUS quiescent galaxies, and the maximum is (15, 0.95) from PRIMUS SF (w/M* selection). The ranges are 0.15 Å for PRIMUS quiescent galaxies, 0.7 Å for PRIMUS SF galaxies, 0.75 Å for PRIMUS SF (w/M* selection), and 0.6 Å for QSO-galaxy (w/M* selection). Clusters are identified at \(R_\perp \approx 15\) kpc with \(W_{2796}\) values between 0.8-0.95 Å and at \(R_\perp \approx 100\) kpc with \(W_{2796}\) values around 0.2 Å. No significant anomalies are observed, as the data points follow the expected declining trend with radius.

The chart titled "Detection and Isolation Index (Test Dataset)" is a line chart that includes a legend indicating the following data series: "Diagram of IOs" in black, "w1" in red, "w2" in green, "w3" in blue, and "w4" in yellow. While labels are not specified, the black line, representing the "Diagram of IOs," consistently overlaps with the other series, with peaks and patterns aligning with the red, green, blue, and yellow lines during certain intervals. The horizontal segments of the lines differ in positions on the y-axis. Peaks are observed in all series at aligned time points, while other parts of the series show deviations in amplitude. The horizontal resting segments may represent threshold-like behaviors for the respective data series. The x-axis represents time in seconds, ranging from 0 to 150 seconds, and the y-axis represents OWMA-VMD(3), ranging from 0 to 1.4. 

For the data values, for the red line (w1), the initial value is 0.48 at 0 seconds, it reaches approximately 0.85 at around 75 seconds, and returns to 0.48 at 150 seconds. The green line (w2) starts at 0.52 at 0 seconds, peaks at approximately 0.90 at around 75 seconds, and ends at 0.47 at 150 seconds. The blue line (w3) begins at 0.54 at 0 seconds, has a middle value of approximately 0.82 at around 75 seconds, and finishes at 0.52 at 150 seconds. The yellow line (w4) starts at 0.55 at 0 seconds, reaches about 0.95 at around 75 seconds, and ends at 0.53 at 150 seconds. The "Diagram of IOs" (black line) initiates at 0 at 0 seconds, rises to approximately 0.92 at around 75 seconds, and remains at 0 at 150 seconds.

In terms of extremum, the series display the following: w1 has a minimum value near 0 and a maximum of approximately 1.24 at around 75 seconds, w2 also has a minimum around 0 and a maximum of approximately 1.15 at around 75 seconds, w3's minimum is near 0 with a maximum around 1.08 at approximately 45 seconds, and w4 has a minimum around 0 and a maximum of approximately 1.10 at around 75 seconds. The "Diagram of IOs" has its minimum value around 0 and a maximum of approximately 0.92 at around 75 seconds.

The ranges for the data series are as follows: w1 ranges from 0 to 1.24, w2 ranges from 0 to 1.15, w3 ranges from 0 to 1.08, w4 ranges from 0 to 1.10, and the "Diagram of IOs" ranges from 0 to 0.92.

The bar chart, which features stacked horizontal bars representing various .onion domain names, includes categories such as facebookcorewwwi.onion, torprojectqyqhjn.onion, torproz4wqyqhjn.onion, and expyuzz4wqyqhjn.onion. The legend consists of five categories indicating levels of difficulty: Very Difficult (red), Somewhat Difficult (light red), Neutral (gray), Somewhat Easy (blue), and Very Easy (dark blue). It is observed that facebookcorewwwi.onion has the highest proportion of responses categorized as "Very Easy" and "Somewhat Easy" compared to the other domains, while expyuzz4wqyqhjn.onion and torproz4wqyqhjn.onion exhibit higher proportions of "Very Difficult" and "Somewhat Difficult" responses. Additionally, Neutral responses are consistently small across all categories. The X-axis is labeled "Responses," ranging from approximately -400 to +400, while the Y-axis lists the four .onion domain names vertically. The approximate data counts reveal that facebookcorewwwi.onion received 15 Very Difficult, 20 Somewhat Difficult, 0 Neutral, 180 Somewhat Easy, and 200 Very Easy responses; torprojectqyqhjn.onion received 80 Very Difficult, 120 Somewhat Difficult, 100 Neutral, 90 Somewhat Easy, and 40 Very Easy responses; torproz4wqyqhjn.onion received 180 Very Difficult, 160 Somewhat Difficult, 40 Neutral, 20 Somewhat Easy, and 10 Very Easy responses; and expyuzz4wqyqhjn.onion received 280 Very Difficult, 140 Somewhat Difficult, 20 Neutral, 15 Somewhat Easy, and 5 Very Easy responses. The maximum value recorded in the chart is 280 (Very Difficult for expyuzz4wqyqhjn.onion), while the minimum is 0 (Neutral for facebookcorewwwi.onion), indicating a total response range across all categories from 0 to 280.

The chart, which is a scatter plot and line chart, illustrates different series of points and lines that show varying rates of increase with respect to "Learning Capacity (C)." The steepness of the series varies, with the blue series being steeper and the brown series exhibiting a more gradual increase. Positive correlations between "Learning Capacity (C)" and "Test Loss (U)" are observed, with the steepness differing across the series. Data points are dispersed, with a higher density at lower values of "Learning Capacity (C)," and the overall spread is skewed, showing more variation in "Test Loss (U)" at higher "Learning Capacity (C)." The X-axis represents "Learning Capacity (C)" on a logarithmic scale, with a range of approximately 0 to 10^3, while the Y-axis represents "Test Loss (U)" on a linear scale, with a range of 0 to 2.0. Series 1 (Dark Blue) has points at (0, 0.6), (500, 1.1), and (1000, 1.6); Series 2 (Orange) has points at (0, 0.4), (500, 0.5), and (1000, 0.55); Series 3 (Green) includes (0, 0.2), (500, 0.35), and (1000, 0.5); Series 4 (Red) is at (0, 0.15), (500, 0.25), and (1000, 0.4); Series 5 (Purple) has (0, 0.1), (500, 0.15), and (1000, 0.25); and Series 6 (Brown) shows (0, 0.1), (500, 0.1), and (1000, 0.1). The maximum value is observed at (0, 1.9) in the dark blue series, while the minimum appears in multiple points around (0-1000, 0.1) in the brown series. The range for the dark blue series is 0.4-1.9, for the orange series is 0.2-1.1, for the green series is 0.2-0.7, for the red series is 0.1-0.5, for the purple series is 0.1-0.3, and for the brown series is 0.1-0.15. There is a dense cluster at low capacity (0-100) and low loss (0.1-0.5), along with multiple parallel trending clusters following distinct trend lines. Notably, there are several outlier points in the dark blue series around 1.7-1.9 test loss, and scattered points are observed above the main trend lines in the orange and green series. Information regarding the chart title, legends, and labels is not provided.

The chart, which is a line chart and scatter plot, illustrates the behavior of three data series over epochs on a linear scale for the x-axis (ranging from 0 to 12) and a logarithmic scale for the y-axis (ranging from 10^-6 to 10^0) in terms of residual values. The blue circles represent Series 1 and exhibit larger oscillations between data points, showcasing a clear periodic peak-and-valley pattern, with initial, middle, and end values of (0, 1.0), (6, ~2x10^-5), and (7, ~8x10^-7) respectively; this series reaches its maximum at (0, 1.0) and minimum at (7, ~8x10^-7), with a range of ~10^-6 to 1.0. Series 2, represented by orange squares, shows smoother declines, with values of (0, 1.0), (6, ~5x10^-4), and (11, ~2x10^-6) in the initial, middle, and end stages respectively, having a maximum of (0, 1.0) and a minimum of (11, ~2x10^-6), also covering a range of ~10^-6 to 1.0. Series 3, denoted by green dashed with stars, also exhibits smooth distributions with initial, middle, and end values of (0, 1.0), (6, ~2x10^-4), and (12, ~2x10^-6) respectively, reaching a maximum of (0, 1.0) and a minimum of (12, ~2x10^-6), with a range of ~10^-6 to 1.0. All series display an overall decreasing trend in residual values as epochs increase, accompanied by a non-linear decrease and varying oscillations; clusters are noted at epoch 0 (all series ~1.0), at epoch 2 (~10^-2), and between epochs 4-6 (~10^-4). Additionally, periodic spikes across all series reflect a sawtooth pattern, with Series 1 showing more pronounced drops between spikes compared to the other series. The black stars marking Series 3 maintain position near each other but are closer to the green series. Legends and labels are not specified, and the chart title is not specified.

The scatter plot presents data from three distinct treatments identified by different legends: black squares represent electropolishing, red squares indicate + anodizing with a thick 100nm oxide, and blue squares denote + electropolishing with the thick oxide removed. It shows that for the same E_acc values, the surface resistance for the red series (with thick oxide) is higher than for the blue series (oxide removed), while the black series values (electropolishing) are generally lower than the red series but higher than the blue series for mid-range E_acc values. The analysis reveals that surface resistance correlates with E_acc in a U-shaped pattern across all three groups, and there is an inverse relationship between quality factor and surface resistance, where a higher quality factor aligns with a lower surface resistance. The x-axis illustrates E_acc (MV/m) within a range of approximately 0-30, while the left y-axis displays surface resistance (nΩ) ranging from approximately 1-8, the right y-axis features the quality factor ranging from 0.5x10¹¹ to 2.5x10¹¹, and the top axis represents the number of photons, spanning from 5.0x10²⁴ to 1.5x10²⁵. The minima for electropolishing occurs at (~8, ~2.8), anodizing at (~8, ~5), and electropolishing removed at (~8, ~3), while maxima are found for electropolishing at (~3, ~3.8), anodizing at (~25, ~7.5), and electropolishing removed at (~25, ~6.8). The surface resistance ranges are approximately ~2.8-3.8 nΩ for electropolishing, ~5-7.5 nΩ for anodizing, and ~3-6.8 nΩ for electropolishing removed. The data displays dense clustering for all series between 5-15 MV/m, with convergence of data points around 8-10 MV/m for minimum values. There is a sharp increase in resistance above 15 MV/m for the anodizing and electropolishing removed series, accompanied by a notable gap, referred to as the "difference due to thick oxide," between anodized and other treatments around 10 MV/m.

The line chart, which has three series represented by legends, displays the Mean Squared Error (MSE) for two models, \( G_1 \) and \( G_2 \), and includes a calculation for Twice the MSE of \( G_1 \). The blue line indicates the MSE of \( G_1 \), the black line represents the MSE of \( G_2 \), and the red line denotes Twice the MSE of \( G_1 \). The black line (\( G_2 \)) begins at a higher value than both the blue line (\( G_1 \)) and the red line (Twice \( G_1 \)), showing the most significant decrease early on. Throughout the entire chart, the blue line (\( G_1 \)) consistently remains lower than both the black and red lines. The red line (Twice \( G_1 \)) is consistently higher than the blue line (\( G_1 \)), approximately double its magnitude. An early convergence is observed for both \( G_1 \) and \( G_2 \) as their values decrease rapidly in the initial epochs, while the black line (\( G_2 \)) stabilizes above the red line (Twice \( G_1 \)) after these initial rapid changes. Meanwhile, the blue line (\( G_1 \)) becomes stable and constant following an initial drop, appearing unaffected by further epochs. The X-axis of the chart represents the epoch, ranging from 0 to 100, and the Y-axis depicts the MSE, ranging from 0 to 0.4. The initial MSE values are 0.05 for \( G_1 \), 0.4 for \( G_2 \), and 0.1 for Twice \( G_1 \). At the middle of the epoch, \( G_1 \) measures 0.04, \( G_2 \) measures 0.09, and Twice \( G_1 \) measures 0.08. By the end of the epoch, both \( G_1 \) and Twice \( G_1 \) remain at 0.04 and 0.08 respectively, while \( G_2 \) stabilizes at 0.09. The minimum MSE values for \( G_1 \), \( G_2 \), and Twice \( G_1 \) are recorded at 0.04, 0.07, and 0.08 respectively, with maximum values of 0.05, 0.4, and 0.1 correspondingly. Thus, the range for the MSE of \( G_1 \) is 0.04 to 0.05, for \( G_2 \) is 0.07 to 0.4, and for Twice the MSE of \( G_1 \) is 0.08 to 0.1.

The data correlations and trends indicate that retained hydrous phases decrease sharply soon after formation in all scenarios but at variable rates depending on radius and formation time; smaller planetesimal radii (e.g., \(R1\)) correlate with better retention of hydrous phases for extended periods, while larger radii (\(R3\)) deplete faster. The chart titled "ILD Preliminary Light score" is a line chart that presents various series for background rejection based on light efficiency, with a range of 0.0 to 1.0 on the x-axis and y-axis. The data shows that the "Full PID" series demonstrates the highest background rejection at all light efficiency values among the compared methods, while performance decreases progressively from "PID < 30 GeV" to "PID < 20 GeV", "PID < 10 GeV", and "No PID" according to their AUC values. The "LCFIPlus OTag" series has the lowest performance among methods, only slightly above the diagonal "Random chance" line. An inverse relationship is evident between background rejection and light efficiency as the curves slope downward, with higher AUC values correlating with better rejection performance. Initial points for all series start at (0.0, 1.0), middle points approximated at x=0.5 are: Full PID (0.5, 0.90), PID < 30 GeV (0.5, 0.88), PID < 20 GeV (0.5, 0.87), PID < 10 GeV (0.5, 0.85), No PID (0.5, 0.83), LCFIPlus OTag (0.5, 0.75), and Random chance (0.5, 0.50). All series converge near (1.0, 0.0), and the background rejection ranges from 0.0 to 1.0 for all curves. Labels for the axes are not specified.

The line chart, while not specified by title, illustrates the test accuracy performance of four series: Iterative Mag.Pr (solid blue line), Iterative Mag.Pr (continued) (dotted blue line), CS (solid purple line), and CS (all runs) (solid green line), plotted against the percentage of weights remaining on a logarithmic scale from 100.0% to 1.9% on the X-axis, and a linear scale of test accuracy ranging from 82% to 94% on the Y-axis. The data comparison indicates that the green line (CS all runs) generally maintains higher accuracy compared to other series across most of the percentage range of weights remaining, with initial accuracy points of approximately 90.5% for Iterative Mag.Pr, 91.2% for CS, and 91.2% for CS (all runs). The blue lines (Iterative Mag.Pr and Iterative Mag.Pr continued) display lower accuracy, particularly as the percentage of weights remaining decreases, with accuracy dropping to about 82.2% at 1.9% weights. The purple line (CS) often performs intermediate between Iterative Mag.Pr and CS all runs, starting at approximately 91.2% and declining to about 87.5% at the end point. All series show a general decline in test accuracy as the percentage of weights remaining decreases, and this decline appears steeper for the Iterative Mag.Pr (both solid and dotted blue lines) relative to the CS series, particularly for lower percentages of weights remaining. The maximum accuracy values are reached by Iterative Mag.Pr at approximately 90.8% (at ~51.4% weights), CS at approximately 91.8% (at ~51.4% weights), and CS (all runs) at approximately 91.8% (at ~51.4% weights). The minimum accuracy values occur at 1.9% weights, with Iterative Mag.Pr dropping to approximately 82.2%, while both CS and CS (all runs) drop to approximately 87.5%. The line chart titled "MRPC" illustrates the approximation error of five methods—Nystrom, SMS-Nystrom, StaCUR(s), StaCUR(d), SiCUR, and SkelApprox—against the proportion of the dataset chosen as landmark samples, which ranges from 0.0 to 1.0 on the X-axis, while the average approximation error ranges from 0.0 to 0.5 on the Y-axis. The error for Nystrom starts high at approximately 0.38 and decreases gradually, remaining relatively flat compared to the others; SMS-Nystrom maintains a flat trajectory with a higher error of about 0.28 relative to the other methods. Both StaCUR(s) and StaCUR(d) exhibit similar patterns, with their errors decreasing slowly and closely tracking each other, starting around 0.42 and 0.37, respectively, at the initial points. SiCUR demonstrates a significant decrease, achieving the lowest error of around 0.10 at the middle point and approximately 0.02-0.05 at the endpoint. SkelApprox starts with limited data points, showing a high initial error before the decline aligns with StaCUR(s) and StaCUR(d). Overall, as the proportion of the dataset increases, the approximation error tends to decrease for all methods; SiCUR exhibits the steepest decline, indicating better performance with an increased dataset proportion, while SMS-Nystrom shows minimal change in error as the dataset proportion increases. At the endpoints (x=1.0), all methods converge to an error between approximately 0.02 and 0.05. The maximum error values at the starting points are around 0.42 for StaCUR(s) and 0.43 for StaCUR(d), while the minimum error values for all methods at the endpoint are about 0.02-0.05. The range of errors varies for each method, with Nystrom showing a range of approximately 0.36, SMS-Nystrom 0.26, StaCUR(s) 0.40, StaCUR(d) 0.41, SiCUR 0.28, and limited data points for SkelApprox indicating a range of around 0.08. A positive correlation exists between the arrival rate and average delay for all three series, though the rate of delay increase varies significantly: MaxWeight grows the fastest, WSLU the slowest, and Delay-based falls in between. The chart titled "Precision/Recall (Cosmos Columns)" is a scatter plot that includes a legend with series names and associated colors: AutoTagRegex-Global (purple), AutoTagRegex (blue), FlashProfile (red), SQLServerPatterns (green), Xsystem (cyan), and Potter’s Wheel (orange). The data comparison reveals that AutoTagRegex-Global (purple) data points are clustered in the top-right corner, indicating high precision and high recall, while AutoTagRegex (blue) demonstrates a downward spread with high precision but varying and relatively lower recall compared to purple. FlashProfile (red) and Potter’s Wheel (orange) overlap closely, suggesting similar precision and recall, whereas SQLServerPatterns (green) displays a slight downward spread, balancing precision and recall without reaching extreme values. Xsystem (cyan) has precision akin to purple but consistently lower recall. The characterization of distribution shows that data points generally exhibit varying spans, from concentrated groupings like purple to elongated patterns like blue. Data correlations indicate an inverse relationship in several cases, notably with blue and green, where higher precision corresponds to lower recall within the given data series. The X-axis represents precision in a range of approximately 0.94 to 1.0, while the Y-axis displays recall ranging from 0 to 1.0. Notably, the AutoTagRegex-Global has a value of approximately (1.0, 0.95), AutoTagRegex around (1.0, 0.85), FlashProfile ranges from (0.99, 0.50) to (1.0, 0.40), SQLServerPatterns varies from (0.94, 0.65) to (0.97, 0.53), Xsystem ranges from (0.95, 0.21) to (0.97, 0.07), and Potter's Wheel is around (1.0, 0.38). The minimum precision value is observed in SQLServerPatterns at 0.94, while the minimum recall value is seen in Xsystem at 0.07. The maximum precision is represented by multiple series at 1.0, and the maximum recall value is found in AutoTagRegex-Global at 0.95. The range for each series is as follows: AutoTagRegex-Global is ~0.95-0.95, AutoTagRegex is ~0.85-0.85, FlashProfile is between 0.40-0.50, SQLServerPatterns ranges from 0.53-0.65, Xsystem varies from 0.07-0.21, and Potter’s Wheel is ~0.38-0.38. The clusters identified include a high precision and high recall cluster (AutoTagRegex variants) at approximately (1.0, 0.85-0.95), a high precision and medium recall cluster (FlashProfile, Potter's Wheel) at approximately (1.0, 0.38-0.50), and a lower precision with a declining recall trend in SQLServerPatterns from (0.94-0.97, 0.53-0.65). Anomalies in the data are notable: Xsystem shows significantly lower recall values compared to other series, whereas AutoTagRegex variants exhibit notably higher recall values at maximum precision. The line chart compares the AUC values of four methods—FM (red triangles), FNN (green squares), DeepFM (blue circles), and PIN (purple diamonds)—across different embedding sizes of 10, 20, 40, and 80. For small embedding sizes, specifically at size 10, FM exhibits a lower AUC of 77.8, which improves to 79.1 at size 20 but declines significantly to 76.8 at size 80. In contrast, PIN consistently achieves the highest AUC across all embedding sizes, with values of 80.1 at size 10, 80.2 at size 20, 80.2 at size 40, and peaking at 80.3 at size 80. FNN and DeepFM show relatively similar performance, both starting at 79.8 for size 10 and maintaining this constant AUC through size 80, with DeepFM slightly decreasing to 79.6 at size 80, while always remaining lower than PIN. The overall trend shows that PIN maintains a stable and high performance, FM peaks at size 20 before a sharp decline, and FNN and DeepFM exhibit minor fluctuations without substantial changes across the embedding sizes. The AUC values range approximately from 77.0 to 80.5 on the Y-axis. The extremum values for FM are a minimum of 76.8 at size 80 and a maximum of 79.1 at size 20; FNN has a constant AUC of 79.8 across all sizes; DeepFM has a minimum of 79.6 at size 80 and a maximum of 79.8 at both sizes 10 and 40; and PIN has a minimum of 80.1 at size 10 and a maximum of 80.3 at size 80. The ranges of AUC values are as follows: FM ranges from 76.8 to 79.1, FNN shows no range, DeepFM ranges from 79.6 to 79.8, and PIN ranges from 80.1 to 80.3.
